nativeRange.habitat: Cultivar of the Borneo-endemic Bucephalandra; in the wild the genus grows as a rheophyte attached to rocks and submerged wood along the banks and beds of fast-flowing rainforest streams

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to
good flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
compact size suits foreground or midground placement
stable, established tank — do not introduce into newly cycled systems

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Bucephalandra Needle Leaf is a compact Borneo cultivar with narrow, wavy, deep-green leaves speckled with white dots. Like all Bucephalandra, it attaches to hardscape rather than planting in substrate, and grows very slowly in low to medium light.
careNotes:
Bucephalandra Needle Leaf is a small cultivar of Bucephalandra, a genus of aquatic aroids endemic to the island of Borneo. It is distinguished from broader Bucephalandra cultivars by its narrow, elongated, slightly wavy leaves — the feature that gives it the 'Needle Leaf' name. Like all Bucephalandra, the plant grows by means of a creeping rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which both leaves and roots emerge). This rhizome must be attached to a hard surface such as lava rock, driftwood, or slate using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line. DO NOT BURY THE RHIZOME in substrate, as covering it generally causes it to rot.

Water conditions in the 22–28 °C range and pH 6.0–7.5 suit the plant well. Lighting is flexible — low light supports steady survival growth, while medium light tends to bring out richer color and the characteristic iridescent shimmer the genus is known for. High light without CO2 injection tends to encourage algae on the slow-growing leaves rather than improved plant growth. CO2 injection is optional but generally produces visibly faster growth and stronger coloration. The plant draws nutrients from the water column, so substrate type is largely irrelevant; light liquid fertilization is sufficient.

Growth is very slow, commonly producing one new leaf every two to four weeks in low-tech setups. The compact adult size and foreground or midground habit make this cultivar well suited to nano tanks and shrimp tanks, as well as a low-growing accent on decorative hardscape.

Newly imported tissue-culture or emersed-grown specimens commonly go through a period sometimes called 'buce melt', in which the original above-water-grown leaves shed after submersion. This is normal: as long as the rhizome stays firm and remains white or pale green inside, new submerged-form leaves will emerge over the following weeks. This plant should be introduced only into established, stable aquariums with settled water chemistry.
breedingNotes: The standard method of propagation in the hobby is rhizome division. Once the plant has established itself and produced new leaves and roots, the rhizome can be cut at a natural bend using clean, sharp scissors. Each division should be left with several leaves and visible roots before being reattached to a fresh piece of hardscape. Divisions of roughly 4 cm of rhizome are typically recommended for plants of this genus.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source. Provides cultivar-specific size (3-5 cm overall, leaves 1-2 cm), leaf shape/color (deep green speckled white, wavy edges, reddish new rhizome growth), and tissue-culture origin (farm-raised, clean of algae/snails).
